2604.18724.title: Beyond One Output: Visualizing and Comparing Distributions of Language Model Generations
2604.18724.paper_summary: Users typically interact with and evaluate language models via single outputs, but each output is just one sample from a broad distribution of possible completions. This interaction hides distributional structure such as modes, uncommon edge cases, and sensitivity to small prompt changes, leading users to over-generalize from anecdotes when iterating on prompts for open-ended tasks. Informed by a formative study with researchers who use LMs (n=13) examining when stochasticity matters in practice, how they reason about distributions over language, and where current workflows break down, we introduce GROVE. GROVE is an interactive visualization that represents multiple LM generations as overlapping paths through a text graph, revealing shared structure, branching points, and clusters while preserving access to raw outputs. We evaluate across three crowdsourced user studies (N=47, 44, and 40 participants) targeting complementary distributional tasks. Our results support a hybrid workflow: graph summaries improve structural judgments such as assessing diversity, while direct output inspection remains stronger for detail-oriented questions.
2604.18724.paper_summary_zh: 使用者通常透過單一輸出與語言模型互動並進行評估，但每個輸出僅是廣泛可能完成的分佈中的一個樣本。這種互動隱藏了分佈結構，例如模式、不常見的邊緣案例以及對小提示變化的敏感性，導致使用者在針對開放式任務迭代提示時過度概括。根據一項與使用語言模型的研究者（n=13）進行的形成性研究，我們探討了隨機性在實踐中的重要性、他們如何推理語言的分佈，以及當前工作流程的問題，我們引入了GROVE。GROVE是一個互動式可視化工具，將多個語言模型生成表示為文本圖中的重疊路徑，揭示共享結構、分支點和聚類，同時保留對原始輸出的訪問。我們在三項眾包用戶研究中進行評估（N=47、44和40名參與者），針對互補的分佈任務。我們的結果支持一種混合工作流程：圖形摘要改善了結構判斷，例如評估多樣性，而直接輸出檢查在細節導向問題上仍然更強。
2604.18724.author: Emily Reif et.al.
2604.18724.authors: Emily Reif, Claire Yang, Jared Hwang, Deniz Nazar, Noah Smith, Jeff Heer
